stimulus: Lithium-ion batteries based on graphite anodes account for roughly 90% of the cells used in electric vehicles, despite recovering only about 350 milliampere-hours per gram of charge capacity. In a study addressing this relative ceiling, a team led by Mei Zhang demonstrated that adding a thin coating of silicon nanowires—which buffer rapid lithium ingress that pure graphite cannot accommodate, whereas graphite stably hosts the steady-state lithium population—allows the cells to recover 450 milliampere-hours per gram or more. Assuming the team’s analysis is correct, cells made with only silicon nanowires, however, are no more durable over hundreds of charge cycles than graphite-only cells. It’s reasonable to conclude, then, that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: battery cells with only silicon nanowires and no graphite would likely deliver more than 450 milliampere-hours per gram in long-term use.
practical electric-vehicle batteries will remain elusive until lithium-ion cells are replaced by a fundamentally different technology.
researchers should evaluate whether minerals other than silicon could similarly improve graphite-based cells.
cells combining graphite and silicon nanowires recover more capacity because each material handles a different phase of lithium uptake.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text says silicon nanowires buffer rapid lithium ingress while graphite stably hosts the steady-state population. Assuming the team’s analysis is correct, the combination outperforms either material alone because each handles a different phase of lithium uptake.

Choice A is incorrect because the text directly contradicts it: silicon-only cells aren’t more durable than graphite-only cells, and the text gives no basis for inferring a higher capacity for them. Choice B is incorrect because the text describes an improvement to lithium-ion cells, not a need to replace the technology entirely. Choice C is incorrect because the text never raises other minerals as candidates for evaluation.
